meta.title: A COVID Journal: An Infectious Diseases Pharmacist's Pandemic Chronicle
meta.author: Steven Smoke
meta.description: Structured chapter data extracted from the Covid-19 Timeline document for use in a React-based interactive website.

chapters.title: The Before Times
chapters.subtitle: From First Reports to First Cases
chapters.mood_description: A calm before the storm. Casual dismissal of early warning signs, life proceeding normally — Rutgers basketball, work trips, grocery runs.

clinical_nodes.label: First Cases
clinical_nodes.content: First reported cases of Covid-19 in Wuhan, China.

clinical_nodes.label: WHO Bulletin
clinical_nodes.content: Novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) outbreak noted on Twitter / WHO.

clinical_nodes.label: First US Case
clinical_nodes.content: First confirmed US case reported in Washington State.

clinical_nodes.label: ID / Infection Control Meeting
clinical_nodes.content: Novel coronavirus (nCoV-2019) officially renamed Covid-19. Topics: employee travel screening, mask shortage, testing protocols.

clinical_nodes.label: Italy: The West's First Major Outbreak
clinical_nodes.content: Italy reports a rapidly escalating outbreak — the first in a western country with reliable reporting infrastructure. Unlike prior outbreaks (Zika, Ebola, SARS, MERS), this one is not contained to one country or region. The coverage is unlike anything people have seen before: overwhelmed hospitals, doctors making impossible triage decisions. A turning point in understanding what is coming.

clinical_nodes.label: Puerto Rico Work Trip
clinical_nodes.content: Extended discussion with a Population Health physician during a flight delay about openness to travel amid the impending Covid-19 wave. He poses a hypothetical: would I travel in May?

clinical_nodes.label: First NJ Case
clinical_nodes.content: First confirmed New Jersey case reported in Fort Lee.

personal_nodes.label: Austria Honeymoon
personal_nodes.content: My wife and I travel to Austria for our honeymoon — Vienna, Salzburg, and Zell am See. Three weeks before the first reported Covid-19 cases in Wuhan.

personal_nodes.label: AMS Prep Meeting
personal_nodes.content: An ID physician colleague mentions coronavirus might be a big deal. I dismiss it as unlikely and off-agenda.

personal_nodes.label: Dismissal
personal_nodes.content: I see the WHO coronavirus bulletin on Twitter and don't think much of it — mentally file it with prior outbreaks (Zika, Ebola, SARS, MERS) that never really spread substantially.

personal_nodes.label: Rutgers Basketball
personal_nodes.content: Rutgers beats #21 Illinois. Program on track for first NCAA tournament berth in 30 years.

personal_nodes.label: My Wife Prepares
personal_nodes.content: While I'm in Puerto Rico, my wife begins preparing for a potential Covid-19 wave — first grocery haul and cleaning supplies.

assets_wishlist: image10 — Opening/title graphic (large landscape image, likely a banner or logo)
image6 — Washington state first case era photo
image24 — Rutgers basketball win photo
image29 — Puerto Rico trip photo (composite/collage style, portrait orientation)

chapters.title: The Run Up
chapters.subtitle: March 9–13, 2020: The Week Everything Changed
chapters.mood_description: Five days of cascading cancellations, first treatment emails, and the dawning realization that this is real. The world of sports collapses in real time.

clinical_nodes.label: First Remdesivir Email
clinical_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer sends the first corporate email discussing remdesivir. Position: do not advocate without clinical trial data, but IND compassionate use is reasonable for highest-risk, critically ill patients.

clinical_nodes.label: ID / IC Meeting — A Warning Issued
clinical_nodes.content: An ID physician argues the system is moving too slowly: 'We need to prepare for what transmission will be, not what it currently is. We will be overrun.' The next 3 meetings are canceled.

clinical_nodes.label: First ID Pharmacy Collaborative Email
clinical_nodes.content: I send the inaugural email to the ID Pharmacy Collaborative sharing a treatment Google doc. Key assessments: Tamiflu unlikely to work; Remdesivir under NIH study; Chloroquine shows 'promising but questionable' evidence from China; Lopinavir/ritonavir retrospective shows no efficacy. I anticipate 'constantly changing evidence.'

clinical_nodes.label: Call for Standard Algorithm
clinical_nodes.content: I email the AMS Chair, the Chief Pharmacy Officer, and Corporate Clinical Pharmacy leadership: 'We need a standard treatment algorithm.'

clinical_nodes.label: First Draft Treatment Protocol
clinical_nodes.content: Our ID PharmD colleague sends a draft treatment protocol — revised 3-4 times in one day. Two pathways: one for ID-managed patients, one for surge overflow. Criteria: ICU / supplemental O2 / high-risk. Preferred: remdesivir compassionate use, then hydroxychloroquine. If ICU: sarilumab via trial or tocilizumab.

clinical_nodes.label: Pediatric ID Physician Remdesivir Inquiry
clinical_nodes.content: The Pediatric ID physician asks about the process to obtain remdesivir for pediatric use.

personal_nodes.label: Distracted by Covid
personal_nodes.content: I'm supposed to be prepping for an ID society meeting presentation but can't focus — convinced this will be a major problem after seeing community transmission data from Washington State.

personal_nodes.label: NBA Positive Test
personal_nodes.content: An NBA player tests positive; the NBA season is suspended.

personal_nodes.label: Big Ten Tournament Canceled
personal_nodes.content: The B10 tournament is canceled while Rutgers is on the court warming up. The NHL season is also suspended. The Rutgers tournament dream dies.

personal_nodes.label: NCAA March Madness Canceled
personal_nodes.content: NCAA March Madness officially canceled.

personal_nodes.label: ID Society Meeting Canceled
personal_nodes.content: The state infectious diseases society cancels its meeting.

assets_wishlist: image37 — Misc photo from ~March 9 (landscape, medium size)
image12 — Photo associated with March 12 Big Ten / NHL cancellations

chapters.title: Exponential Growth
chapters.subtitle: The Hydroxychloroquine Wars
chapters.mood_description: A frantic three weeks of evolving guidelines, drug shortages, political interference, Twitter misinformation, and inter-physician conflict over HCQ dosing. The science is moving faster than anyone can track.

clinical_nodes.label: Ibuprofen Controversy
clinical_nodes.content: The French Health Minister tweets that ibuprofen could be an aggravating factor. Anecdotal and pharmacological speculation, not evidence-based. WHO temporarily recommends against ibuprofen; WHO and EMA later retract on 3/19.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Stockpile Revealed
clinical_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer shares that our health system has stockpiled hydroxychloroquine and requests an algorithm for distribution.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Protocol Version 3
clinical_nodes.content: Our ID PharmD colleague sends updated recommendations: HCQ for most eligible patients, remdesivir via clinical trial if mechanically ventilated, IL-6 antagonist if ARDS. The AMS distributes the first formal guidance (Version 3).

clinical_nodes.label: Steroids Debate Begins
clinical_nodes.content: I share a JAMA retrospective suggesting steroids may help; CDC and WHO recommend against them at this time. Later proven to reduce mortality.

embed.label: Read the Study in JAMA Internal Medicine

clinical_nodes.label: First Staff Pharmacist Presentation
clinical_nodes.content: I give my first Covid-19 presentation to staff pharmacists. Packed room. Most questions cannot be answered.

clinical_nodes.label: NEJM: Lopinavir/r Not Effective
clinical_nodes.content: NEJM RCT published: lopinavir/ritonavir not effective for Covid-19. Removed from future protocol versions.

clinical_nodes.label: French HCQ Study Circulates
clinical_nodes.content: An ID physician shares the French HCQ/azithromycin study — not yet published or peer-reviewed. Debate begins on dosing, azithromycin addition, and QTc monitoring. The President calls HCQ 'a game changer' at a press conference. Azithromycin goes on ASHP shortage.

embed.label: Read the Original Preprint PDF (IHU Marseille)

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Use 'Out of Control'
clinical_nodes.content: I note in an email: 'well our use is starting to get out of control.' First daily HCQ tracking reports begin.

clinical_nodes.label: Vitamin C and Zinc: Evidence-Free Request
clinical_nodes.content: A corporate pharmacy director asks about including Vitamin C and zinc comments in the guideline. After clarification, the request is not based on any evidence — just questions that have come up. A common theme at this stage of the pandemic.

clinical_nodes.label: First Compassionate Use Approved
clinical_nodes.content: My first Gilead compassionate use remdesivir request, submitted on behalf of an ICU physician, is approved.

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab Allocation Debate
clinical_nodes.content: ID pharmacists debate the criteria for tocilizumab use. Concerns that the criteria are not specific enough — but there is no clear answer for who it should go to. Limited supply adds urgency. A recurring theme of the pandemic: allocating an unproven drug when both the evidence and the stock are thin.

clinical_nodes.label: President Tweets About HCQ
clinical_nodes.content: The President tweets that hydroxychloroquine and azithromycin could be 'one of the biggest game changers in the history of medicine,' bringing massive public attention and intensifying pressure to prescribe both drugs.

clinical_nodes.label: CBMC Pharmacy Updates to ID Begin
clinical_nodes.content: I begin sending regular updates to the ID division: case numbers, HCQ utilization, medication inventory, and literature summaries. Sent three days a week for the next month and a half.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir Trial Site: Ambition Without Infrastructure
clinical_nodes.content: Physicians frequently raise the idea of becoming a remdesivir clinical trial site and developing our own trial. In practice, there is a shortage of primary investigators with trial experience and our research infrastructure is limited. We do not become a remdesivir site. A handful of trials are approved through IRB, but very few patients are enrolled.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Reporting: Wildly Wrong Numbers
clinical_nodes.content: A system-level HCQ report shows just 14 patients across the entire system — meanwhile CBMC alone has 37. Daily manual reconciliation of HCQ numbers is required for corporate reporting throughout most of the first wave.

clinical_nodes.label: ACEi/ARB as Solution: Early Misstep
clinical_nodes.content: A forwarded email from a board-certified critical care pharmacist (via ACCP CC PRN) argues that because ACE2 is the SARS-CoV-2 receptor, ACE inhibitors and ARBs could be a therapeutic solution. These medications are later proven not to be effective.

clinical_nodes.label: CAP Antibiotics Added to Covid-19 Order Set
clinical_nodes.content: The decision is made to include community-acquired pneumonia antibiotics in the Covid-19 order set. Antibiotic overuse for Covid-19 becomes a massive problem in 2020, likely contributing to significant increases in antibiotic-resistant infections. Doxycycline is prioritized for CAP treatment to conserve the dwindling supply of azithromycin.

clinical_nodes.label: Testing Delays at Peak PUI Volume
clinical_nodes.content: Covid-19 test turnaround time is 3–5 days. This is the peak of persons under investigation — patients held in limbo, consuming isolation resources, while the system waits for results.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Duration Curtailed
clinical_nodes.content: Greater than 5-day HCQ duration discouraged due to limited evidence and supply constraints.

clinical_nodes.label: Azithromycin Recommendation Challenged
clinical_nodes.content: My ID PharmD colleague and I draft an SBAR to pull azithromycin from the protocol citing: no prior evidence, non-equivalent control group, no clinical benefit demonstrated, and potential QTc toxicity.

embed.label: Read the Analysis on Contagion Live

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Dosing Conflict
clinical_nodes.preview: An ID physician emails hospital leadership demanding higher HCQ dosing based on a non-comparative French study. A heated email exchange and phone call follow.
clinical_nodes.content:
An ID physician (Smith) emails the CEO, CMO, and Chair of Medicine demanding higher HCQ dosing based on a retrospective, non-comparative French follow-up study. He raises supply concerns about the current regimen, makes stronger claims about the data — "Since patients on this regimen will clear the virus very quickly…" — and implies he might be blocked from using the French dosing, which was not the case. He also follows up with kind words about the residents, as workload for them had been a prior point of discussion.

I respond by email emphasizing the major limitations: the latest study is non-comparative, only 15% of patients were on oxygen, and significant questions remain about the original study. Given pharmacokinetics, a loading dose with shorter duration makes sense. Better evidence supports our regimen, though all evidence remains limited. Supply is a major concern. I commit to advocating for a shorter duration and close with gratitude for everyone's work.

Smith calls immediately, saying I "stabbed him in the back." As with most conversations with him, he drifts into other topics and the tension dissipates.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA Emergency Use Authorization
clinical_nodes.content: FDA issues EUA for chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir Compassionate Use
clinical_nodes.content: Gilead approves compassionate use remdesivir for a pregnant patient in the ICU. An emergency C-section is performed in the ICU before the drug arrives.

embed.label: Read the Story in the Washington Post

clinical_nodes.label: "There Are No Rules Anymore"
clinical_nodes.preview: A day of unproven therapies: ribavirin, zinc, colchicine, cyclosporine. Steve captures it: "There are no rules anymore."
clinical_nodes.content: A wide-ranging day of unproven therapies. I add ribavirin to the reported inventory based on a contact of an ID physician in Egypt. Zinc sulfate shortages require active management — not recommended, not proven, but used widely. A conversation with my ID pharmacy colleague covers zinc + HCQ, colchicine, and cyclosporine: the first time I have heard of colchicine for Covid-19, the first time he has heard of cyclosporine. Steve captures the moment: "There are no rules anymore."

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab Outcomes Concerning
clinical_nodes.content: Suspicion that tocilizumab is being used too late — very few positive outcomes observed.

clinical_nodes.label: Hub-and-Spoke ICU Strategy
clinical_nodes.content: A hub-and-spoke strategy is established for ICU services in response to a massive surge in critically ill patients. ICU, Anesthesia, Pediatrics, and Burn services all take over adult Covid-19 ICUs.

clinical_nodes.label: Universal Masking Begins
clinical_nodes.content: Universal masking goes into effect at CBMC.

clinical_nodes.label: Guidelines Updated Again
clinical_nodes.content: Updated guidelines: HCQ duration extended to 5-7 days, tocilizumab criteria broadened, anticoagulation and steroids formally addressed.

clinical_nodes.label: First Baricitinib Question
clinical_nodes.content: A pharmacist at NBI asks about baricitinib — my first encounter with it as a potential immunomodulator for Covid-19. Speculation about its efficacy had appeared in a Lancet study. It is proven effective months later.

embed.label: Read the Study in The Lancet

clinical_nodes.label: IRB Chair: Trial Enrollment vs. Compassionate Use
clinical_nodes.content: The IRB Chair asks about our tocilizumab inventory. He only wants to recruit to the sarilumab study if tocilizumab is unavailable — because his study has a placebo arm. An interesting stance that cuts to a difficult ethical tension of the pandemic. Option A: enroll a patient in a randomized clinical trial, where they may be assigned to drug or placebo. Option B: decline trial enrollment and simply give them the unproven drug. Option A moves us closer to an answer. Option B does not. No patient deserves judgment for decisions made under the stress of severe illness. But if COVID-19 persists, we will only know which treatments work because of patients who chose Option A — and chose to drive medicine forward for the greater good.

personal_nodes.label: A Friend's 50th Birthday Skipped
personal_nodes.content: After mutual friends decided not to attend, I email a friend that my wife and I won't attend a 50th birthday gathering due to Covid-19. Our friend's parents later end up in the ICU; her father passes away.

personal_nodes.label: Ibuprofen: Impossible to Disprove
personal_nodes.content: This blows up on social media over the next few weeks, especially after the WHO picks up on it. It became a frequent question at work and outside of work with friends and family. A single premature shout of warning — shared, echoed, and bounced around the world in hours. Level-headed questions like 'what's the source for this claim?' got lost in the noise. Proving the absence of evidence is impossible, while the provocative rumor spreads faster than a virus. And the corrections and retractions never travel with the intensity of the original report. When this cycle repeats over and over, the result is a flood of misinformation.

personal_nodes.label: My Wife Works From Home
personal_nodes.content: My wife begins working from home. Initially the expectation is that it will be a few weeks. Five years later, she's never gone back.

personal_nodes.label: Reading Chinese Papers
personal_nodes.content: 'Trying to read all of these Chinese papers' — I use Google Translate on foreign-language studies to keep up with global evidence.

personal_nodes.label: NJ Closes Nonessential Businesses
personal_nodes.content: New Jersey closes nonessential businesses.

personal_nodes.label: Emails From Strangers
personal_nodes.content: I receive an email from a new attending physician about the guidelines. Over the following weeks, I receive emails from physicians in NYC and India — both unaffiliated with our health system — because the guidelines included my contact info.

personal_nodes.label: Celebrating a Preprint With Amy
personal_nodes.content: A preprint RCT from China suggests benefit with hydroxychloroquine. I celebrate the news with Amy. This is my first real encounter with preprints — a format I become very familiar with over the course of the pandemic. Preprints come from good intentions: rapid sharing of study results. But there is a price to skipping the steps that ensure the slow but forward march of scientific progress. That price is confusion, when the important studies get mixed with those that are far more suspect — and that confusion is magnified when preprints of questionable merit are shared widely by media outlets. I never confirm whether this paper was ever published in a peer-reviewed journal.

personal_nodes.label: The Offensive Coordinator
personal_nodes.content: My role does not have me on the front lines, at the bedside. I work more like a football team's offensive coordinator — except less controlling and more advisory. One of my tasks is to work with a diverse group of experts to provide formal treatment recommendations. We face two opposing pressures: respect the importance of evidence-based medicine, where no practice should be recommended without adequate evidence — and recognize that the pandemic will not wait for high-quality trials. The surge of patients is here, now. By any nonpandemic standard, there is not adequate evidence to recommend for or against many COVID-19 therapies. But this nonanswer provides no help to the clinicians on the front lines, swamped with patients, looking for some kind of direction.

personal_nodes.label: Oval Office Meeting
personal_nodes.content: An ID physician meets with the President, a TV host, and the FDA Director to pressure HCQ authorization.

personal_nodes.label: Video Call With My Wife
personal_nodes.content: My wife and I sat on our balcony and talked for 30 minutes. We knew it was worth documenting, so we recorded it. My wife is grocery shopping online. The niece and nephew are 'living their best life making mud soup.' My wife's knuckles are cracked from hand sanitizing. I'm not yet wearing a mask at work — the CDC hadn't recommended it for healthcare workers yet. 'We're just a covid hospital, every unit is an ICU, no one knows how to treat it, people want to try everything.' 'My music selection is a good indicator of my mental state — I've been listening to emo like it's 2005.'

chapters.title: The Dark Ages
chapters.subtitle: The Surge, the Silence, and the Trailers
chapters.mood_description: The hospital empties of non-essential staff but fills with the dying. Empty hallways. Refrigerated trailers in the parking lot. Clap-outs. 'Here Comes the Sun.' Tooth-grinding stress. An overwhelming sense of hopelessness on the morning commute.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Skepticism Grows
clinical_nodes.content: A NEJM quasi-experimental HCQ study circulates. The first serious discussion in the ID pharmacy group of pulling the HCQ recommendation entirely. The group notes that stopping azithromycin 'wasn't as bad as expected.'

clinical_nodes.label: Ivermectin: First and Last (for 8 months)
clinical_nodes.content: Two ID physicians separately ask about ivermectin. I explain that concentrations required for efficacy are orders of magnitude above what can be safely achieved. No more ivermectin questions for eight months.

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab: Earlier May Be Better
clinical_nodes.content: Preliminary tocilizumab results suggest earlier administration may be more effective — consistent with our clinical suspicion.

clinical_nodes.label: NIH Guidelines Adopted
clinical_nodes.content: Our AMS decides guidelines will mirror NIH guidelines going forward — requiring a much higher evidence threshold before making recommendations. No recommended therapies at this point.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Pulled From Guidelines
clinical_nodes.content: The HCQ recommendation is officially removed from our guidelines based on NIH and IDSA guidance.

clinical_nodes.label: Infection Paradox: CDI Down, Fungal Infections Up
clinical_nodes.content: Hospital CDI (C. diff) rates plummet due to dramatically improved hand hygiene across the facility. Meanwhile, fungal infections, MRSA, and other hospital-acquired infections rise due to the surge in critically ill patients with prolonged stays. The same pattern holds for respiratory illnesses more broadly — influenza, RSV, and rhinovirus transmission collapse as masking and social distancing suppress their spread. Other infectious diseases would remain suppressed throughout the pandemic.

clinical_nodes.label: All Hands on Deck
clinical_nodes.content: As the surge overwhelms the hospital, everyone steps outside their training. The pediatric intensivist becomes an adult intensivist. The investigational drug pharmacist becomes a critical care pharmacist. Medical students are called into action in areas of care they were never prepared for. More and more is asked of healthcare workers — and each time, they step up to the task. Not because they want to, but because they have to, for their patients.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir: Contradictory Data
clinical_nodes.content: A Chinese remdesivir RCT in Lancet shows no benefit. The same day, NIAID shares (unpublished, non-peer-reviewed) data from its RCT indicating benefit. Timing causes considerable confusion. But the Lancet study also fell short of its projected patient enrollment — the study was underpowered. Drawing definitive conclusions from an underpowered study is like calling a football game at the end of the third quarter. Congratulations to the 49ers, Super Bowl LIV champions — they were up 10, it was over, right? Wrong. There was still a quarter left to play.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA issues emergency use authorization for remdesivir.

clinical_nodes.label: "Do We Have Remdesivir? If Not, Why Not?"
clinical_nodes.preview: The US government distributes remdesivir — no RWJBH hospitals are selected. A reporter contacts CBMC on behalf of a patient: "Do we have remdesivir? If not, why not?"
clinical_nodes.content:
The US government begins distributing remdesivir — and no RWJBH hospitals are selected for the initial shipment. We are told it was sent to the hardest-hit areas, but it is unclear how most of New Jersey was passed over. Two NJ sites received it; both returned their allocations to the state, which assembled a committee to determine redistribution.

A reporter from ABC, working on behalf of a patient, reaches out to CBMC Public Relations. The inquiry is directed to Pharmacy and to me through the IRB Chair: "Do we have remdesivir? If not, why not?"

clinical_nodes.label: Anakinra Early Data
clinical_nodes.content: First retrospective cohort data on anakinra published in The Lancet. I note it 'looks better than toci data.' Tocilizumab use is dropping significantly.

embed.label: Read the Study in The Lancet Rheumatology

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ NEJM Study: No Benefit
clinical_nodes.content: A large observational study published in NEJM finds no benefit from hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: What Is Our Threshold to Act Before National Guidelines?
clinical_nodes.preview: I ask the CPO and AMS physician lead: what is our threshold to act before national guidelines? The answer: we follow national guidelines — a decision shaped by the HCQ experience.
clinical_nodes.content:
I email the RWJBH CPO and AMS physician lead: "What is the threshold for modifying our recommendations before national guidelines?" The answer: we will follow national guidelines.

The question anticipates a recurring problem — new data that shifts the treatment landscape days or weeks before guidelines catch up. This will eventually come to a head most visibly with tocilizumab, leaving clinicians wanting to act on emerging evidence with no system guidance to support them.

The answer, in some ways, is likely a response to the early HCQ experience: a lesson in the dangers of moving fast on unproven therapies, and the institutional cost of having to walk recommendations back once national guidelines catch up.

clinical_nodes.label: First Remdesivir Shipment
clinical_nodes.preview: 26 courses arrive at 6–7pm after a day-long delay. Allocation strategy debated, supply falls short of demand, and a Saturday morning CMO call is needed to approve orders.
clinical_nodes.content:
We are told to expect the first remdesivir shipment. The CEO emails pharmacy leadership and me to sort out receiving logistics. We ask the CMO how to allocate a limited supply — 4–15 courses expected — against high demand of 25–30 anticipated eligible patients.

The CMO responds: first come, first served — and this becomes the system-wide strategy. Around this time I hear from colleagues at other institutions that first come, first served is not considered equitable. We did not have the capacity to implement a more equitable allocation process.

Midday, the shipment is reported delayed. The next day, 26 courses of remdesivir arrive around 6–7pm. An ID physician pre-emptively enters roughly 10 orders that evening — after I have left for the day. We never established a clear implementation process. I end up calling the CMO on Saturday morning to review and approve the orders.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Gated to Clinical Trial
clinical_nodes.content: A P&T memo to Medical Staff states: HCQ for Covid-19 must go through a clinical trial. Non-trial use permitted only if a patient is ineligible. The ID physician responds calling it 'Tuskegee type illegal.'

personal_nodes.label: Life During the Surge
personal_nodes.preview: Empty hallways, refrigerated trailers, clap-outs, tooth-grinding, and a hotel stay to protect my family. The human texture of the worst weeks.

sections.title: The Hospital Empties
sections.text: Non-essential personnel work from home, leaving the hospital eerily quiet — empty hallways, a silent cafeteria.

sections.title: Community Donations
sections.text: All kinds of donations arrived. Hand sanitizer from local distilleries that had converted their production lines. Food, drinks, snacks. Mask comfort adapters. Handwritten letters from entire classrooms of kids who had never met any of us. There was a period where you could feel the appreciation from the community — everyone wanted to do something, anything, to help. It meant something.

sections.title: The City Goes Quiet
sections.text: Outside the hospital, the world shut down. Parks, playgrounds, schools — all closed.

sections.title: Trailers in the Parking Lot
sections.text: The hospital morgue fills. Two refrigerated trailers are placed in the physician parking lot for overflow bodies. They remained for months. Every employee passed them going in and out each day.

sections.title: 'Here Comes the Sun'
sections.text: The Beatles' 'Here Comes the Sun' plays over the hospital loudspeaker each time a patient is extubated. The sound quality is described as horrendous.

sections.title: Clap-Outs
sections.text: Staff line the halls and applaud patients discharged after weeks- or months-long admissions.

sections.title: Hotel Stay
sections.text: I move into a hotel from April 7 to April 18 to protect my family from exposure.

sections.title: Tooth-Grinding
sections.text: I notice mild, persistent tooth pain — eventually realizing it is from clenching my jaw. A subconscious physical response to facing unanswerable questions with lives on the line.

sections.title: Morning Commute Grief
sections.text: 'I remember getting emotional a few times. It was always on my morning commute... I remember feeling an overwhelming hopelessness. Not just for myself and all the patients and their families. But for everyone I care about and how isolated and scared they must be.'

sections.title: My Wife's Knit Mask
sections.text: My wife handknits a mask for me to use outside the hospital, since there weren't enough for non-healthcare workers. 'It probably didn't work that well but it meant a lot to me that she worked on it for me.'

sections.title: My Wife Cuts My Hair
sections.text: My wife cuts my hair — three attempts, two hours — and it turns out well. I have jealous coworkers.

personal_nodes.label: Healthcare Heroes Salute
personal_nodes.content: A procession of thanks is held outside St. Barnabas Medical Center — police, fire, and community members lining up to honor hospital staff during the height of the surge. We got a bonus and a pin that said 'COVID-19 Heroes.' Healthcare heroes signs on front lawns, letters and posters from classrooms, donations of food and sanitizer from local businesses — everyone wanted to do something, anything, to help. And it meant something. But singling out one person felt wrong when everyone was going above and beyond. Yet by not celebrating individual heroics, the recognition felt hollow. It wasn't enough to truly honor what was happening inside those walls.

personal_nodes.label: Preparing for the Next Wave
personal_nodes.content: CBMC pharmacy featured in a Wall Street Journal article on hospitals stocking up on Covid-19 drugs ahead of a feared second wave in the fall.

embed.label: Read the Article in the Wall Street Journal

assets_wishlist: image5 — Photo from ~April 9 area (small portrait, likely hospital/clinical)
image35 — Photo from April 11 area (medium landscape)
image19 — Photo from April 11 area (small portrait, likely ivermectin-era)
image44 — Photo from April 13
image39 — Photo from April 30 (medium portrait)
image40 — Photo from April 29 area (wide landscape)
image20 (x2) — Email/memo screenshots associated with April 2 and the HCQ trial gating memo

chapters.title: The MAB Graveyard
chapters.subtitle: The Monoclonal Antibody Arms Race
chapters.mood_description: A two-year arc of approved monoclonal antibodies — each arriving with promise, each eventually rendered obsolete by viral mutation. A clinical arms race the virus ultimately wins.

clinical_nodes.label: Sarilumab Fails Primary Endpoint
clinical_nodes.content: Regeneron reports the sarilumab study fails to meet its primary endpoint. Criteria was too broad, including circumstances where the drug is ineffective. Later proven effective in more targeted use. Regeneron pivots to a monoclonal antibody targeting the spike protein.

embed.label: Read the Coverage on Clinical Trials Arena

clinical_nodes.label: First Compassionate Use: Casirivimab/Imdevimab
clinical_nodes.content: I work with the Pediatric ID physician on the first (of at least two) compassionate use requests for Regeneron's casirivimab/imdevimab in a pediatric patient.

clinical_nodes.label: The President Receives Casirivimab/Imdevimab
clinical_nodes.content: The President publicly discloses he has Covid-19 and receives casirivimab/imdevimab via compassionate use. This brings considerable media attention to the medication.

embed.label: Read the Story on STAT News

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for bamlanivimab. Immediate discussions about where to administer it — a pharmacy colleague and I suggest the ED. The system decides on EDs by November 16.

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab Dispensing Begins
clinical_nodes.content: Our hospital begins dispensing bamlanivimab.

clinical_nodes.label: Casirivimab/Imdevimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for casirivimab/imdevimab (Regeneron). Both 'bam' and 'Regeneron' are used simultaneously based on inconsistent supply.

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab/Etesevimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the combination. Rationale: resistance to bamlanivimab suspected in circulating variants; etesevimab intended to restore activity. Variant monitoring becomes essential to treatment decisions.

clinical_nodes.label: Mutation Reckoning
clinical_nodes.content: I reflect on my prior dismissal of mutation concerns: 'This was very wrong.' My first concern was the Alpha variant (UK), more transmissible but not affecting MAB activity. Then variants emerged worldwide.

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab Alone EUA Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: EUA for single-agent bamlanivimab revoked due to variant resistance.

clinical_nodes.label: Sotrovimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for sotrovimab.

clinical_nodes.label: Casirivimab/Imdevimab and Bamlanivimab/Etesevimab EUAs Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: Both EUAs revoked due to variant resistance.

clinical_nodes.label: Bebtelovimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for bebtelovimab despite 'basically no clinical data.'

clinical_nodes.label: Sotrovimab EUA Restricted
clinical_nodes.content: Sotrovimab EUA revised to exclude areas with high BA.2 prevalence, including New Jersey. Later fully revoked.

clinical_nodes.label: Bebtelovimab EUA Revoked — The Virus Wins
clinical_nodes.content: Bebtelovimab, the last available monoclonal antibody, has its EUA revoked. 'The virus has won the arms race.'

personal_nodes.label: MAB Paper Submitted
personal_nodes.content: My MAB paper is submitted to the Journal of Medical Virology after rejection from many journals.

personal_nodes.label: MAB Paper Published
personal_nodes.content: My MAB paper is officially published in the Journal of Medical Virology.

embed.label: Read the Paper in the Journal of Medical Virology

assets_wishlist: image1 — Large wide graphic at the end of MAB section (full-page width image)
image41 — Photo after MAB timeline (medium landscape)
image38 — Photo after MAB timeline (medium landscape)
image27 — Photo after MAB timeline (medium landscape)

chapters.title: Vaccination Peregrination
chapters.subtitle: From 50% Threshold to 300 Shots
chapters.mood_description: A rare chapter of optimism in a dark year. The arrival of vaccines — 90% efficacy news that blew everyone away — and the shared joy of vaccination clinics. Pharmacists give hope a shot.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA Vaccine Efficacy Threshold
clinical_nodes.content: FDA publishes guidance for industry: a Covid-19 vaccine must demonstrate at least 50% efficacy to receive authorization.

clinical_nodes.label: A Pharmacy Leader Cautions Against Vaccine
clinical_nodes.content: A prominent pharmacy administrator emails a large listserv urging extreme caution: recommends waiting for full FDA approval PLUS 6 months of post-marketing data before vaccinating. Advises pharmacists to carry liability insurance.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA: Two Months Safety Data Required
clinical_nodes.content: FDA announces it requires two months of safety data before issuing any vaccine EUA, effectively eliminating the possibility of pre-election approval.

clinical_nodes.label: Vaccine Task Force Formed
clinical_nodes.content: First email from the Chief Pharmacy Officer about an internal vaccine task force formation.

clinical_nodes.label: Pfizer 90% Efficacy Announced
clinical_nodes.content: Pfizer vaccine trial reports 90% efficacy — a result that stuns experts across the field.

clinical_nodes.label: I Watch the FDA VRPAC Meeting
clinical_nodes.content: I watch most of the FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee meeting on the Pfizer vaccine. I will watch many more VRPAC and ACIP meetings over the next year.

embed.label: View the FDA VRBPAC Meeting Page

clinical_nodes.label: Pfizer EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the Pfizer-BioNTech Covid-19 vaccine.

clinical_nodes.label: I Get My Pharmacist Immunizer License
clinical_nodes.content: I obtain my pharmacist immunizer license.

clinical_nodes.label: Moderna EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the Moderna Covid-19 vaccine.

clinical_nodes.label: First Vaccines at the Hospital
clinical_nodes.content: First day of vaccine administration at our hospital.

clinical_nodes.label: I Start Vaccinating
clinical_nodes.content: My first day administering vaccinations. Initially staff only, then the public. I provide over 300 vaccinations over the next four months at our hospital, a county vaccination site at a former mall, and an elementary school gym.

personal_nodes.label: NEJM Podcast Revelation
personal_nodes.content: I start listening to the NEJM Interviews podcast — weekly Covid-19 literature updates. My first realization that vaccine mandates might happen, that this would be the biggest vaccination campaign in history, and that I need to get licensed to vaccinate.

embed.label: Watch the NEJM Covid-19 Interviews Playlist on YouTube

personal_nodes.label: Missed the Tweet
personal_nodes.content: I was upset I missed the Pfizer 90% efficacy tweet — a resident was the one who told me. I had been checking Twitter 2-4x per day all year.

personal_nodes.label: My Turn: Vaccinated at 5pm
personal_nodes.content: I'm vaccinated at 5pm — an hour after the clinic is scheduled to close — as the vaccine team looks for volunteers to use up remaining doses. I was not in the first priority group.

personal_nodes.label: Joy at the Vaccine Clinic
personal_nodes.content: 'I remember the shared sense of positivity, excitement and joy among the vaccinators and public. Everyone was happier than they had been in nearly a year.'

personal_nodes.label: Notable Vaccinees
personal_nodes.content: I vaccinate the Chair of Medicine and several physicians I work with. I also vaccinate an NHL player and some minor league players. I supervise technicians vaccinating a first-round NHL draft pick.

personal_nodes.label: The CPO Calls Before a TV Appearance
personal_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer calls me 20 minutes before a television appearance asking how to explain how the vaccines work.

assets_wishlist: image21 — Photo from November 9, 2020 (medium portrait, likely Pfizer news era)
image25 — Photo from FDA VRPAC meeting December 10 (wide landscape)
image34 — Photo from Moderna EUA area (medium landscape)
image9 — Photo from first vaccination day, December 21 (medium portrait)
image33 — Photo from vaccination clinic era (small portrait)
image42 — Photo from late December / CPO TV call era (medium landscape)

chapters.title: Fox and Friends
chapters.subtitle: The HCQ Conflict Comes to a Head
chapters.mood_description: A concentrated drama centered on one physician's continued advocacy for hydroxychloroquine after the evidence had turned against it — culminating in a Fox News appearance where I am named by an ID physician, and the COO privately drops f-bombs in my defense.

clinical_nodes.label: Dr. Smith on Fox News
clinical_nodes.content: ID physician Dr. Stephen Smith appears on Fox & Friends stating hydroxychloroquine is "the beginning of the end of the pandemic."

embed.label: Watch the Segment on Fox News

clinical_nodes.label: P&T Memo: HCQ Prohibited
clinical_nodes.content: I work with the CMO, the Pharmacy Director, and the Associate Director on a P&T memo to stop HCQ use for Covid-19. The ID division votes on prohibition — passes by a narrow margin. The ID co-chair is completely unaware of the process; he does not check email.

clinical_nodes.label: CMO vs. ID Physician Over HCQ Ban
clinical_nodes.content: The CMO and the ID physician clash directly over the HCQ ban.

clinical_nodes.label: Fox News Appearance — My Name Used
clinical_nodes.content: The ID physician returns to Fox News claiming his unpublished, retrospective study proves hydroxychloroquine works. He names me by name on national television: 'I turned to the pharmacist, Steve Smoke at St. Barnabas and I said, I got people that are literally more than four times difference in weight, one or both groups is getting the wrong dose.' The study is vastly inferior to available evidence and — to my knowledge — was never published.

embed.label: Watch the Segment on Fox News

clinical_nodes.label: Vindication: UTI Pathway Praised
clinical_nodes.content: The Chair of Medicine sends an email praising my work on an unrelated UTI antimicrobial stewardship pathway to the CEO, CFO, and CMO. The CMO responds in agreement.

clinical_nodes.label: RECOVERY Trial: HCQ No Benefit
clinical_nodes.content: The RECOVERY trial determines no benefit from hydroxychloroquine in hospitalized Covid-19 patients. I distribute the details and clinical trial contact info to ID physicians.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ EUA Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: FDA officially revokes the emergency use authorization for hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: RECOVERY Trial: Dexamethasone Reduces Mortality
clinical_nodes.content: The RECOVERY trial reports dexamethasone reduces mortality — a landmark finding. Questions arise about whether a trial report (not peer-reviewed) is sufficient to change practice. Our system waits for the NIH guideline update but the CPO is pressured by an ID physician to act sooner.

embed.label: Read the RECOVERY Trial Dexamethasone Results

clinical_nodes.label: Ivermectin Returns — Physician Confrontation
clinical_nodes.content: Two ID physicians order ivermectin. I push back. One is very rude: 'How dare you?… I've been practicing medicine for X years…' The physician compares the situation to bamlanivimab. Bamlanivimab is later proven effective; ivermectin is not.

personal_nodes.label: Named on TV
personal_nodes.content: I am named by an ID physician on Fox News in the context of a disagreement over HCQ dosing methodology. The clip and transcript are preserved as external links.

personal_nodes.label: COO Drops F-Bombs
personal_nodes.content: The hospital COO visits my office privately to offer personal support and express frustration with the ID physician.

personal_nodes.label: HCQ Paper Accepted
personal_nodes.content: My hydroxychloroquine paper is accepted for publication (submitted in February 2021, after rejection from several journals).

personal_nodes.label: My Expertise Stops Mattering
personal_nodes.content: Early in the pandemic, friends and family valued my perspective as an infectious disease pharmacist. They called, texted, asked what I was seeing, what they should do. But as politics overtook the pandemic, that changed. My firsthand experience and years of expertise weren't needed anymore — they had other sources of information. The same people who had sought my opinion weeks earlier were now skeptical of what I was telling them. It was a strange and personal betrayal, losing credibility with the people closest to you while walking in and out of the hospital every day.

personal_nodes.label: Opinion Article Published
personal_nodes.content: My opinion article is published in Pharmacy Practice News. Five months into the pandemic, I write about what I've learned: that my most common answer has been the most difficult one — 'I don't know.' That sharing speculation based on flimsy evidence does not bring clarity. That knowing when a question isn't yours to answer is just as important as answering it. And that buried in the devastation are a million stories of heroism. I urge everyone to ask for those stories. Tell those stories. And listen to them.

embed.label: Read the Article in Pharmacy Practice News
